parts.content: Describe how a hydrogen atom becomes excited in a discharge tube.
parts.markscheme:
- State that high-energy electrons in the tube collide with hydrogen atoms.
<marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that this energy transfer causes atomic electrons to transition to higher energy levels.
<marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understand the discharge tube environment
steps.content: In a discharge tube, a high potential difference (voltage) is applied across the gas. This accelerates free electrons within the tube, providing them with significant kinetic energy. To understand how these electrons behave, you can review **Topic E.1: Structure of the atom**, which discusses how atomic models explain the behavior of electrons.

steps.title: Identify the collision process
steps.content:
The first step in the excitation process occurs when these high-energy (fast-moving) electrons collide with the hydrogen atoms present in the tube. 

This is a physical interaction where the free electron passes near or hits the atom, allowing for an exchange of energy. This earns the first mark on the markscheme.

steps.title: Explain the energy transition
steps.content:
During the collision, some of the free electron's kinetic energy is transferred to an electron orbiting the hydrogen nucleus. 

Because energy levels in an atom are discrete (quantized), the atomic electron absorbs exactly the right amount of energy to 'jump' or transition from a lower energy level to a higher energy level. This state is what we call an **excited** atom. This explanation secures the second mark.

parts.content: State what happens when the excited electron returns to a lower energy level.
parts.markscheme:
- State that a photon is emitted.
<marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understand the excited state
steps.content:
In an atom, electrons occupy specific, quantized energy levels. When we say an electron is **excited**, it means it has absorbed energy and moved from its ground state to a higher energy level. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic E.1: Structure of the atom**, which explores how atomic spectra provide evidence for these discrete energy levels.

steps.title: Analyze the energy transition
steps.content:
When the electron "returns to a lower energy level," the atom must lose a specific amount of energy equal to the difference between the two levels ($ \Delta E $). 

According to the law of conservation of energy, this energy cannot simply disappear; it must be released into the environment.

highlights.text: returns to a lower energy level

steps.title: Identify photon emission
steps.content:
The energy lost by the atom during this transition is released in the form of electromagnetic radiation. A single packet of this light energy is called a **photon**.

The energy of this emitted photon is given by the equation found in the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet:

$$E = hf$$

Where:
- $E$ is the energy difference between the levels
- $h$ is Planck's constant
- $f$ is the frequency of the light

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you simply need to identify the result of this process. 

When the electron moves to a lower energy level, **a photon is emitted**.

parts.content: Calculate the energy of a photon with a wavelength of 486 nm.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$: 
$E = \dfrac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34})(3.00 \times 10^8)}{486 \times 10^{-9}}$
<marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final answer:
$4.09 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$
<marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify formula and constants
steps.content:
To calculate the energy of a photon, we use the combined photon energy equation derived from two relations in your data booklet: $E = hf$ and $c = f\lambda$. Combining these gives us:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

From the **Physical Constants** section, we have:
- Planck’s constant, $$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$$
- Speed of light, $$c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Convert wavelength to meters
steps.content:
The wavelength is given in nanometers (nm). Since our constants use meters, we must convert the wavelength to SI units (meters) before substituting into the formula.

$$1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

$$\lambda = 486 \text{ nm} = 486 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate energy
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values for $h$, $c$, and $\lambda$ into the energy equation. This step earns the first mark ($M1$).

$$E = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^{8})}{486 \times 10^{-9}}$$

Calculating this gives the final energy in Joules. Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$E = 4.09 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression using scientific notation (2nd [EE]).

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the photon energy using the wavelength in meters.

steps.title: Convert wavelength to meters
steps.content:
Before we start our calculations, we must ensure our units are consistent with the SI units used in the data booklet. The wavelength is given in nanometers (nm), which we need to convert to meters (m).

$$1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

So, for the given wavelength:

$$486 \text{ nm} = 486 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the frequency
steps.content:
Using the wave equation found in **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**, we can find the frequency ($f$) of the photon. Since light travels at the speed of light ($c$), we replace the velocity $v$ with $c$.

$$v = f\lambda$$

Rearranging for frequency:

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

Substitute the values for the speed of light ($c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) and our wavelength:

$$f = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8}{486 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f \approx 6.17 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the speed of light by the wavelength in meters.

desmos.instructions: Type the division into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the frequency of the light.

steps.title: Calculate the photon energy
steps.content:
Now that we have the frequency, we use the Planck equation from **Topic E.1 (Structure of the atom)** to determine the energy ($E$) of the photon.

$$E = hf$$

Substitute Planck's constant ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$) and the frequency we just calculated:

$$E = (6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (6.17 \times 10^{14})$$

$$E \approx 4.09 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply Planck's constant by the frequency result from the previous step.

calculator.description: Calculate the energy using Planck's constant.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The energy of a single photon at this wavelength is $4.09 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn one mark (**M1**) for showing the correct substitution into the formulas and one mark (**A1**) for the final numerical answer with correct units.

parts.content: Outline how the hydrogen line spectrum supports the idea of quantized energy levels.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the spectrum consists of discrete/specific lines (wavelengths).
<marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that this implies only specific energy differences exist, confirming energy levels are quantized.
<marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify discrete wavelengths
steps.content: The first thing to notice is the nature of the hydrogen spectrum. It is not a continuous rainbow; instead, it consists of **discrete wavelengths**. This means that only very specific colors of light, such as the one at **486 nm**, are emitted by the hydrogen atoms.

steps.title: Calculate photon energy
steps.content:
Every line in the spectrum represents a photon of a specific frequency. Using the formula for photon energy from your data booklet:

$$E = hf$$

Because the wavelengths are fixed (discrete), the frequencies are also fixed. This implies that the photons emitted by the atom can only have **specific energy values**. For example, a 486 nm photon has a specific energy of approximately $4.09 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the energy of a photon with a wavelength of 486 nm using $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$.

steps.title: Relate to energy levels
steps.content:
Photons are emitted when an electron transitions (drops) from a higher energy state to a lower one. The energy of the photon matches the **difference** between these two states ($E_{photon} = \Delta E$). 

Since we only observe specific photon energies, there must be only **specific energy differences** possible within the atom. This confirms that the energy levels are **quantized** (fixed), rather than continuous. 

This concept is a core part of **Topic E.1: Structure of the atom**, which explores how these discrete patterns provide evidence for atomic models.

steps.title: Observe the discrete lines
steps.content: When we look at the light emitted by hydrogen, we don't see a continuous rainbow of colors. Instead, we see a **line spectrum**, which consists of light at **discrete wavelengths** (such as the specific value of 486 nm mentioned in the question).

steps.title: Contrast with continuous spectra
steps.content: To understand why this is important, imagine if energy levels were *not* quantized. If an electron could have any amount of energy (like a ball on a smooth ramp), it could lose any amount of energy when it drops to a lower state. This would produce a **continuous spectrum** containing every possible wavelength.

steps.title: Relate energy to wavelength
steps.content:
From the data booklet, we know that the energy of a photon is related to its frequency (and therefore its wavelength):

$$E = hf$$

Because we only observe specific wavelengths, it means only specific amounts of energy are being released by the atoms. This is direct evidence that electrons can only move between specific, fixed energy states.

steps.title: Confirm quantized levels
steps.content: The existence of these distinct lines proves that only **specific energy differences** are possible within the atom. This confirms that the energy levels are **quantized**—the electron can only exist at certain allowed energy levels and nowhere in between.

steps.title: Identify the spectral evidence
steps.content:
First, we look at the experimental observation: the hydrogen emission spectrum is not a continuous rainbow. Instead, it consists of **discrete lines** (specific wavelengths). This is a crucial starting point for understanding atomic structure.

This concept is explored in **Topic E.1: Structure of the atom**, where we look at how atomic spectra provide evidence for the internal arrangement of electrons.

steps.title: Apply the transition model
steps.content:
According to the **Atomic Transition Model**, light is emitted when an electron "jumps" or transitions from a higher energy level to a lower one. The energy of the emitted photon is exactly equal to the difference in energy between these two levels.

From the **Waves** and **Nuclear** sections of your data booklet, we know that the energy of a photon ($E$) is related to its wavelength ($\lambda$) by combining these two formulas:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f\lambda \\ E &= hf \end{aligned}$$

By substituting $f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$ (where $v=c$ for light), we get:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

steps.title: Relate lines to energy
steps.content:
Since we only observe specific wavelengths (like 486 nm), the photons emitted must have only **specific, discrete energies**. 

If the energy levels in the atom were continuous (like a ramp), the electron could release any amount of energy, creating a continuous spectrum. However, because we only see specific "lines," the energy of the electron can only change by specific amounts.

steps.title: Conclude on quantized levels
steps.content:
Because only specific energy transitions are possible, we conclude that the electron can only exist in fixed orbits or states. This confirms that the energy levels within the atom are **quantized**.

To earn full marks on this question, ensure you mention:
1. The spectrum consists of **discrete lines** (A1 mark).
2. This implies only **specific energy differences** exist, meaning energy levels are quantized (R1 mark).

highlights.text: implies only specific energy differences exist, confirming energy levels are quantized

questions.specification: The Bohr model describes the energy levels of a hydrogen atom.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic E - Nuclear and quantum physics > E.1 Structure of the atom > E.1.4 Bohr model and quantized energy levels (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: E.1.4 Bohr model and quantized energy levels (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks for the Bohr-model hydrogen energy level equation, meaning of principal quantum number n, and photon energy for a transition between n=3 and n=2—directly quantized energy levels in the Bohr model.

parts.content: Write down the equation for the energy levels of the hydrogen atom and explain the significance of the quantum number $n$.
parts.markscheme:
Equation:
$E_n = -\frac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$ <marks>A1</marks>

Significance:
$n$ represents the principal quantum number / principal energy level <marks>A1</marks>
It determines the energy of the level and the size/radius of the orbit (higher $n$ means higher energy/larger radius) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall the energy level equation
steps.content:
In the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom, the electron can only exist in specific, quantized energy states. The standard numerical equation used to find the energy of these levels in electronvolts (eV) is:

$$E_n = -\frac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$$

This equation tells us that the energy of the ground state ($n=1$) is $-13.6 \text{ eV}$, and higher levels have energies that approach zero as $n$ increases.

steps.title: Identify the quantum number n
steps.content:
The variable $n$ in the equation is called the **principal quantum number**. 

It can only take positive integer values ($n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$), representing the discrete energy levels (or shells) of the atom. 

This concept is a core part of **Topic E.1: Structure of the atom**, where we look at evidence from atomic spectra for these quantized levels.

steps.title: Explain the significance of n
steps.content:
The value of $n$ is significant because it defines the physical properties of the electron's state:

1.  **Energy**: As $n$ increases, the energy $E_n$ becomes less negative (e.g., from $-13.6$ to $-3.4$), meaning the electron is at a **higher energy level**.
2.  **Radius**: The quantum number also dictates the size of the orbit. A higher $n$ corresponds to a **larger orbital radius**, placing the electron further from the nucleus.

In an IB exam, identifying both the energy and the radius relationship is essential for full marks.

parts.content: Calculate the energy, in joules, of the photon emitted when an electron transitions from the $n=3$ energy level to the $n=2$ energy level.
parts.markscheme:
Substitution to find energy difference:
$\Delta E = E_3 - E_2 = \left(-\frac{13.6}{3^2}\right) - \left(-\frac{13.6}{2^2}\right)$ OR $\Delta E = 13.6\left(\frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\Delta E = 1.89 \text{ eV}$ <marks>A1</marks>

Conversion to joules and final answer:
$E = 1.89 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} = 3.02 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate energy of n=3
steps.content:
To find the energy of the electron at the third level ($n=3$), we use the formula for hydrogen energy levels: $$E_n = -\frac{13.6}{n^2}$$

Substituting $n=3$:
$$\begin{aligned} E_3 &= -\frac{13.6}{3^2} \\ E_3 &= -\frac{13.6}{9} \\ E_3 &\approx -1.511\text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate energy of n=2
steps.content:
Similarly, we calculate the energy for the second level ($n=2$):

$$\begin{aligned} E_2 &= -\frac{13.6}{2^2} \\ E_2 &= -\frac{13.6}{4} \\ E_2 &= -3.400\text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine photon energy in eV
steps.content:
The energy of the emitted photon is the difference in energy between these two levels. 

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta E &= E_3 - E_2 \\ \Delta E &= -1.511 - (-3.400) \\ \Delta E &= 1.889\text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$ 

This rounds to $1.89\text{ eV}$, which earns the first two marks.

calculator.description: Calculate the energy difference between the levels in eV.

steps.title: Convert energy to Joules
steps.content:
To get the final answer in Joules, we convert the energy from eV using the elementary charge constant $e$ from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{joules} &= E_{eV} \times e \\ E &= 1.889 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \\ E &\approx 3.02 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Convert the eV energy difference into Joules.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The energy of the emitted photon is $3.02 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$. This aligns with the transition from a higher energy state to a lower one, where energy is released as light.

This calculation is a key part of **Topic E.1: Structure of the atom**, which explores how quantized energy levels explain atomic spectra.

steps.title: Convert ground state to Joules
steps.content:
In the Bohr model, the energy levels of a hydrogen atom are determined relative to the ground state ($13.6 \text{ eV}$). Our first step is to convert this constant into Joules using the elementary charge from the data booklet.

$$E_{\text{ground}} = 13.6 \text{ eV} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}$$

$$E_{\text{ground}} = 2.176 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the ground state energy in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate energy of each level
steps.content:
The energy of an electron in the $n$-th level is given by $E_n = -\frac{E_{\text{ground}}}{n^2}$. We need to find the energy for levels $n=3$ and $n=2$ directly in Joules.

$$\begin{aligned} E_3 &= -\frac{2.176 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}}{3^2} = -2.42 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \\ E_2 &= -\frac{2.176 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}}{2^2} = -5.44 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate E3 and E2 in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate the photon energy
steps.content:
When an electron transitions from a higher energy level to a lower one, the energy of the emitted photon is equal to the difference between these two levels.

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{photon}} &= |E_3 - E_2| \\ E_{\text{photon}} &= |(-2.4178 \times 10^{-19}) - (-5.44 \times 10^{-19})| \\ E_{\text{photon}} &= 3.0222 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:
$$E_{\text{photon}} = 3.02 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the two energy levels.
